title: Modular Hanging Closet System
summary: Wall-hung 1x12 closet towers on a single level cleat — build shelf and hanging components on the bench, then hook them on the wall like cabinets. No feet on the floor.
description:
The smart part of this system is that nothing touches the floor. Every component — shelf towers, hanging sections — hangs from a 1x3 cleat screwed level into the wall studs, with 1x6 backers at top and bottom screwed through into studs. Hanging beats floor-standing in a closet for three reasons: the baseboard never has to be notched around, the vacuum fits underneath, and one level cleat guarantees every component tops out at exactly the same height no matter how out-of-level the floor is.

Each component is the same five-minute recipe at a different length: two 1x12 sides, shelves pocket-screwed between them (three 3/4" pocket holes per shelf end, no holes in the sides), 1x6 backers top and bottom. The published variants — 1-shelf (18-1/4" sides), 2-shelf (31"), 3-shelf (43-3/4"), 5-shelf (64-1/2"), and a double-hanging section — differ only in side length and shelf count. The cut list here is one 3-shelf, 36"-wide tower, the most useful single component; the variant lengths ride along in the notes.

Shelf width is your choice up to 36" — the shelf boards are always the component width minus 1-1/2" (two 3/4" sides). Use finger-jointed 1x12s if you're painting: they're straighter and cheaper than clear pine and the joints vanish under primer.

The one step you cannot eyeball is the cleat. It must be LEVEL and it must be in STUDS — the cleat carries everything you own that hangs. Remember the components sit 5-1/2" above the cleat, so hang the cleat top at about 76" for a comfortable reach.
timeLabel: 4–6 hrs for a component plus hanging; a weekend for a full closet

tools.note: 3/4" setting, three holes per shelf end. All holes in the shelves, never the sides.
Clean square crosscuts in wide 1x12 stock. A circular saw with a guide works too.
The cleat carries the entire system. Level is not negotiable.
Only if you rip components shallower than 11-1/4" to clear a tight closet.

materials.name: 1x12 finger-jointed pine, 8 ft
materials.note: One 3-shelf 36" tower: sides and shelves. Finger-jointed stock is straight and takes paint well.

materials.name: 1x6, 8 ft
materials.note: Top and bottom backers — two times the closet's overall length when building multiple components.

materials.name: 1x3, 8 ft
materials.note: The wall cleat, full length of the closet.

materials.name: Wood construction screws, 3", self-tapping, star drive
materials.note: Cleat and backers into studs. Every one of these goes into framing, not drywall.

materials.name: Wood dowel, 1-1/4", 4 ft
materials.note: The closet rod — cut to fit between components or sides.

cutList.note: Always the component width minus 1-1/2" — here 36" wide. Shelf count is variant + 1 (top and bottom count).
The top backer doubles as the hanging rail sitting on the cleat.
Full closet length — cut to your wall, 96" shown for an 8-ft closet.

steps.title: Hang the wall cleat — level, into studs
steps.body:
Find the studs and mark them. Hang the 1x3 cleat with its top at about 76" (components will sit 5-1/2" ABOVE the cleat — account for that when choosing height), screwed to every stud it crosses with the 3" screws.

Check level before the second screw and again after the last. Everything you build after this step inherits this line: a 1/4" slope in the cleat is a 1/4" slope in every shelf in the closet.
materials: Wood construction screws, 3", self-tapping, star drive

steps.title: Build the component carcass
steps.body:
Drill three 3/4" pocket holes on each end of every shelf board — no holes in the sides, ever; sides stay clean because they're the visible faces. Screw the shelves between the two sides with 1-1/4" pocket screws and glue, top and bottom shelves flush with the side ends.

Every component in the system is this same operation at a different length. If you're building several, cut and drill everything as one batch, then assemble like a production line.

steps.title: Add the backers
steps.body:
Glue and trim-screw a 1x6 backer across the back at the bottom, and a full 1x6 across the back at the top. The top backer is the structural one — it's what gets screwed through into the studs, and its bottom edge is what registers on the cleat.

Square the carcass against the backers as you fasten: the backers are your squaring panel as well as your hanger.

steps.title: Install the closet rod
steps.body:
For hanging sections, screw the rod sockets to the sides — centered vertically in the hanging space, and set far enough off the wall that a hanger clears (about 10" of clearance to the back; check your deepest hanger against your carcass depth if you ripped the sides down).

Cut the 1-1/4" dowel to fit between the sockets.

steps.title: Hang and lock down the components
steps.body:
Lift each component onto the cleat, slide it to position, and screw through the top 1x6 backer into every stud behind it with 3" screws. A narrow component that doesn't cover a stud gets screwed sideways to its neighbor instead — the system braces itself.

If a component ends the run with an exposed side, trim that end with a 1x2 for a finished edge. Then load it with more clothes than the old wire shelf ever held.
materials: Wood construction screws, 3", self-tapping, star drive

images.alt: Wall-hung modular closet components with shelves and hanging rods
